noHiddenFees.title: No Hidden Fees

0.text: We drove from Jaz Beach to Sveti Stefan on our first morning and couldn't believe the views. Stopped at Bečići for lunch, made it to the Sveti Stefan viewpoint before sunset. The car meant we could dodge the buses and go at our own pace. Would absolutely book again.

1.text: Three of us rented for five nights — Top Hill one evening, Trocadero the next, then a road trip south to Petrovac for a slower afternoon. Having a car meant no waiting for taxis at 2am. The agent at Tivat was quick and the car was spotless. Exactly what we needed.

2.text: We prefer quieter roads and olive groves to beach clubs. The car took us south to Petrovac, through the old village lanes above Budva, and along the coast at our own gentle pace. The old town in the evenings was magical. A very well organised rental from start to finish.

0.q: What documents do I need to rent?
0.a: You'll need a full driving licence with Latin-script details, your passport, and a physical credit card issued in the main driver's name. If your licence is from a country that uses a non-Latin script, bring an International Driving Permit too. Photocopies and digital-only licences are not accepted at pickup.

1.q: Can I drive to Croatia or Albania?
1.a: Yes. Cross-border travel is permitted to Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Kosovo, and Serbia. The Dubrovnik day trip via Debeli Brijeg is one of the most popular runs from Budva — about 2.5 hours each way. Flag the countries at booking and the Green Card is prepared for you.

2.q: What's actually included in the price?
2.a: Third-party liability insurance and CDW come standard on most cars. VAT, a roadside safety kit, and a transparent fuel policy are included too. Mileage terms differ per vehicle — check the car listing. Zero-excess cover and extras like child seats can be added at booking.

3.q: How does pickup work at Tivat Airport?
3.a: Your agent meets you at Tivat Airport arrivals hall. The 22 km coast road to Budva takes around 25 minutes depending on summer traffic. No shuttle, no waiting around — the car is parked right outside the terminal.

4.q: Can I return the car somewhere different?
4.a: One-way rentals are available across 28+ towns and cities in Montenegro. Pick up at Tivat, drop off in Budva, or vice versa — just select different locations when you book.

5.a: Standard vehicles: free cancellation up to 7 days before pickup. Luxury cars and convertibles: free cancellation up to 30 days in advance. A 6% processing fee applies on all cancellations regardless of timing.

6.q: Do I need to pay a deposit?
6.a: It depends on the vehicle. Deposits typically fall between €100 and €500 for most riviera rentals. Some cars require no deposit at all. Where one applies, it sits on the main driver’s physical credit card and is released when the car is returned undamaged.

7.q: Where can I park near Budva Old Town?
7.a: The underground car park by the Old Town walls is closest but fills by mid-morning in summer. The open lot behind the bus station is larger and cheaper. Free street parking exists along the Becici road but requires a 10-minute walk. In peak season, arrive before 9 am for the best spots near the walls.

aboutBody.p1: We started Podgorica Airport Car Rental because we watched too many visitors land at Tivat, squeeze onto a transfer bus, and spend their whole holiday within walking distance of one hotel. Budva deserves more than that. Seventeen beaches stretch along this riviera, rooftop bars overlook a 2,500-year-old walled town, and an hour inland the mountains start. All of it is reachable — but only if you have wheels.
aboutBody.p2: Over 800,000 visitors flood this strip of coast every summer. They come for Mogren's cliff-sheltered pebbles (0.5 km from the old town), Jaz's festival-ready sand (2.5 km west), and Bečići's kilometre-long shore (3 km south). They stay for the food — grilled brancin at a waterfront konoba for under €12. They come back for Top Hill, the 3,000-capacity superclub carved into the cliffs above Budva. A rental car turns a beach holiday into an adventure.
aboutBody.p3: Search our fleet, pick a car, and book online with instant confirmation. We cover the entire riviera plus Tivat Airport (22 km), Podgorica Airport (65 km), and 26 more pickup spots. Fleet runs from budget city cars that slot into old-town parking spaces to SUVs built for Lovćen's switchbacks. Every vehicle carries full insurance with no hidden fees.
aboutBody.promise2: Tivat Airport meet-and-greet at the arrivals door
aboutBody.promise3: Transparent pricing: the number at booking is the number you pay
aboutBody.promise4: Cross-border driving to Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, and Kosovo
aboutBody.promise6: 24/7 roadside backup wherever you end up
aboutBody.p4: Our team lives on the Budva Riviera. We know that Mogren car park fills by 10am in July, that the coast road south to Petrovac gets jammed after 4pm on Saturdays, and that the best cevapi on the strip is at a place most tourists walk straight past. We share this stuff freely — because a better road trip means a better review, and better reviews keep us going.
aboutBody.p5: Question about parking at Jaz? Wondering whether to take the tunnel or the coast road to Kotor? Drop us a message on WhatsApp, Viber, or plain old phone. Or just search the fleet and book — confirmation is instant.

kotorBody.h1: Kotor — 25 km North Through the Tunnel
kotorBody.p1: Leave the beach buzz behind and drive 25 km north through the Sozina-style mountain tunnel. On the other side, the Bay of Kotor opens up — a fjord-like inlet surrounded by limestone peaks. Kotor's walled old town sits at the water's edge, packed with cat-filled lanes, stone churches, and restaurants tucked into medieval arches. This is a different Montenegro entirely.
kotorBody.p2: Budget tip: street parking outside the walls fills fast in summer. The large public lot near the main gate charges around €1 per hour. Arrive before 10am or after 5pm and you'll save time and patience.
kotorBody.stJohnText: The ramparts zig-zag 1,200 steps up the mountain behind old town. At the top, the fortress gives you a sweeping view of the bay, the orange rooftops, and the cruise ships far below. Bring water and good shoes — the stone steps get slippery. Free if you enter from the back trail; €8 from the official gate.
kotorBody.cathedralText: Kotor's most recognised building dates to 809 AD, rebuilt in 1124 after an earthquake. Twin bell towers frame a Romanesque facade. Inside, Byzantine frescoes and a silver-gold altar piece make this one of the most significant churches on the Adriatic.
kotorBody.perastTrip: Continue 15 minutes along the bay to Perast — a one-street baroque village with two islands offshore. A boat to Our Lady of the Rocks costs about €5 return. The tradition of dropping rocks into the bay to grow the island dates to 1452 and still happens every July.
kotorBody.cetinjeTrip: Take the legendary 25-hairpin-bend road up from Kotor to Cetinje, the old royal capital. Stop at each hairpin for increasingly dramatic bay views. The royal monastery houses relics including what is claimed to be the hand of John the Baptist.
kotorBody.royalCourtText: Cetinje's royal palace from 1871 displays art, photographs, and artefacts from Montenegro's short-lived kingdom. Budget €3 for entry. It sits next to the monastery in a quiet town that feels frozen in the 19th century.

budvaBody.h1: Budva — Where the Riviera Comes Alive
budvaBody.p1: Picture this: a peninsula old town ringed by stone walls, cocktail bars on the ramparts, and a beach within sight of every restaurant table. That's Budva. Montenegro's undisputed summer capital pulls over 800,000 visitors a year. A third of all tourism in the country happens right here, on this stretch of coast between Jaz and Sveti Stefan.
budvaBody.p2: The town sits 22 km south of Tivat Airport — a breezy 30-minute drive. Ancient walls by day, Top Hill's 3,000-person dancefloor by night. Budget travellers eat grilled squid at harbour konobas for €8. Big spenders reserve a table at Citadela restaurant inside the fortress. Everyone ends up on the same beaches.
budvaBody.beachesIntro: Twenty-one kilometres of coastline run south from Budva. Every beach has its own character:
budvaBody.mogren: Two connected coves hidden behind a cliff tunnel, 0.5 km from the old town. Pebble shore, dramatic rock walls. Sunbed hire around €15/pair in peak season. Parking at the nearby lot fills by mid-morning — walk from town instead
budvaBody.jaz: A wide sandy bay 2.5 km west. Festival stages go up here in summer. Free entry but parking costs €3–5 depending on how close you get. Arrive early on weekends or you'll park on the road
budvaBody.svetiStefan: The photogenic fortified island turned luxury hotel, 8 km south. The public beach on the mainland side is gorgeous and free. Views from the road above cost nothing
budvaBody.becici: Fine sand stretching 2 km, 3 km south of Budva. Water sports central: jet ski hire from €40/15min, parasailing €35, banana boat rides for the kids. Several beachfront restaurants serve lunch without leaving the sand
budvaBody.oldTownText: Budva's Stari Grad sits on a compact peninsula barely 200 metres across. Narrow lanes twist between Venetian-era buildings, opening into tiny squares with church facades and cafe tables. The Citadela fortress anchors the southern tip — the terrace restaurant up top has sunset views that justify the prices. Below the walls, a wooden boardwalk wraps around to Ricardova Glava beach, the town's smallest and most popular strip of sand.
budvaBody.svetiStefanTrip: Drive 8 km south along the coast road and Sveti Stefan appears on a bend — a cluster of terracotta rooftops on a rocky islet connected by a narrow causeway. Pull over at the viewpoint above for the photo. The mainland beach beside the causeway is open to everyone.
budvaBody.kotorTrip: Head north through the mountain tunnel and you're in Kotor in 30 minutes. UNESCO old town, 1,200 fortress steps, and bay-side restaurants. Continue to Perast for the island churches — add an extra 15 minutes.
budvaBody.lovcenTrip: Turn inland toward Lovćen National Park and climb to the Njegoš Mausoleum at 1,657 metres. On a clear day you can see across four countries from the top. The switchback road from the coast is part of the experience — budget 90 minutes each way.
budvaBody.gettingAroundText: The old town is pedestrian-only — explore it on foot. For the car, use the paid lots near the main gate (€1–2/hour) or the larger car parks along the Jadranski Put main road. In July and August, beach parking fills early. Mogren lot is gone by 10am. Jaz by 11am on weekends. Bečići has more capacity but the access road crawls after noon. Pro tip: hit the further beaches first thing, then come back to town when the afternoon crowd thins out.

perastBody.h1: Perast — Forty Minutes Along the Bay
perastBody.p1: From Budva, drive 35 km north through Kotor and along the bay's edge. The road hugs the waterline, passing stone villages and small harbours until Perast appears — a single row of baroque palaces reflected in still water. Traffic noise drops to almost nothing. This is the Adriatic coast at its most serene.
perastBody.p2: Fewer than 850 people live here year-round. Between 1420 and 1797 the town belonged to Venice, and the Venetian influence shows in every facade, bell tower, and waterfront palazzo. Perast's sailors were legendary — the town produced sea captains who trained the Russian navy under Peter the Great. Today, waterfront konoba restaurants serve fresh-caught fish and local wine while you watch the bay.
perastBody.stGeorgeText: A natural cypress-covered island just offshore. The 12th-century Benedictine monastery is closed to visitors, but the island's dark silhouette against the bay is one of Montenegro's most photographed scenes. Boat captains along the harbour will circle the island for a few euros.
perastBody.ladyOfRocksText: A man-made island built by fishermen dropping rocks and sinking old ships over centuries. The tradition continues every 22 July — locals fill boats with stones and dump them around the island. Inside the church, a tapestry took one woman 25 years to complete using her own hair. Boat rides from the Perast harbour cost around €5 return.
perastBody.historicText: Sixteen churches and several baroque palaces line the waterfront. The Perast Museum occupies Palata Buiovich — widely considered the most elegant building on the Montenegrin coast. Entry is around €5. Model ships, old maps, and portraits of sea captains fill the rooms. Budget 45 minutes if you enjoy maritime history.
perastBody.monasteryText: A short drive north toward Risan brings you to this peaceful monastery. Its collection spans Greek, Russian, and Boka religious art. No entrance fee. Keep driving another 10 minutes to Risan for well-preserved Roman floor mosaics — an unexpectedly impressive stop.

tivatBody.h1: Tivat — Superyachts and Seafood, 22 km Away
tivatBody.p1: Most visitors see Tivat only through the airport window. That's a mistake. The town's waterfront has been transformed by Porto Montenegro — a luxury marina complex where superyachts dock next to cocktail terraces. It is a different planet from Budva's beach-party energy, and it is only 30 minutes up the coast.
tivatBody.p2: The permanent population hovers around 13,000, but summer doubles that. A palm-lined promenade connects the old town to the marina district. Side streets hide family-run restaurants where grilled fish and a carafe of Vranac wine costs half what you'd pay at a marina table. Worth knowing.
tivatBody.beachesText: Seventeen beaches stretch across 3 km of Tivat's coastline. The two standouts:
tivatBody.beach1: Plavi Horizanti (Blue Horizons): white sand, shallow entry, perfect for small kids. Sunbed hire around €10/pair
tivatBody.beach2: Przno on Sveti Marko Island: a former resort island now quietly returning to nature. The water is some of the clearest on the bay
tivatBody.portoText: The marina complex turned Tivat from a quiet naval town into a Mediterranean hotspot. Berths hold superyachts up to 250 metres. On shore: designer boutiques, waterfront restaurants, a pool club, and a naval heritage museum housed in the old submarine repair facility. Summer weekends bring live music and yacht parties. Entry to the marina promenade is free — window shopping is the main sport.
tivatBody.monasteryText: The ruins of St. Michael Archangel Monastery sit on the Prevlaka tip, dating to the 10th century. Ongoing archaeological digs keep uncovering older layers. The peninsula trail takes about 30 minutes and offers bay views that rival anything on the coast.
tivatBody.churchText: Built in 1734, this hilltop church holds religious paintings and local artefacts. The real draw is the view — the bay stretches out below with Perast and the islands in the distance. Free entry.
tivatBody.museumText: If maritime history appeals, Kotor's Maritime Museum is just 10 minutes further along the bay. Centuries of Adriatic seafaring packed into one beautiful building.

tivatAirportBody.h1: Grab Your Car at Tivat Airport (TIV)
tivatAirportBody.p1: Tivat Airport sits 22 km from Budva — that is about 30 minutes on a straight coast road. The recently modernised terminal handles summer charters alongside year-round Belgrade flights. It is small, efficient, and built for getting you out fast.
tivatAirportBody.p2: Summer is peak season with direct flights from across Europe. Winter slows to a trickle, but daily Belgrade connections keep the airport ticking year-round. The code is TIV.
tivatAirportBody.dist1: Budva: 22 km — about 30 minutes on the coast road
tivatAirportBody.dist2: Kotor: 8 km — roughly 10 minutes through the bay
tivatAirportBody.dist3: Podgorica: 80 km — just over an hour inland
tivatAirportBody.dist4: Sveti Stefan: 30 km — 35 minutes heading south
tivatAirportBody.facilitiesText: Compact but functional: a cafe, duty-free shop, souvenir stands, and a bureau de change. Buses run to Herceg Novi and Budva, and taxis wait outside. But with your own car, none of that matters — you walk straight through to the car park.
tivatAirportBody.whyText: Tivat is the closest airport to the riviera. Period. Within 30 minutes of wheels down, you can be on Budva's main drag looking for a parking spot near the old town. No transfer bus. No taxi negotiation. No wasted first afternoon. Your holiday starts at the terminal door.
tivatAirportBody.meetText: Our agent meets you in the arrivals hall with a name board. Your car is parked steps away. Average time from baggage belt to driving: about 10 minutes.

podgoricaBody.p1: Montenegro's capital sits an hour inland from the riviera. Most tourists skip it entirely, heading straight for the coast. That is understandable — but it means missing a genuinely interesting city that shows you a completely different side of the country.
podgoricaBody.p2: The architecture tells the story: Ottoman-era bridges, socialist apartment blocks, glass-fronted shopping centres, and the cable-stayed Millennium Bridge all share the skyline. It is messy and real. The cafe culture is excellent. Locals drink espresso for hours along Hercegovačka street, and nobody is in a rush.
podgoricaBody.p3: Budget tip: restaurant prices in Podgorica run about 30-40% less than Budva's tourist strip. A full grilled meat platter with sides and local beer costs around €8–10 at a neighbourhood konoba.
podgoricaBody.seeText: Sat-Kula, the Ottoman clock tower, still stands in the old Stara Varoš quarter. The Duklja archaeological site dates back roughly 2,000 years. For shopping, the Delta City mall covers mainstream brands while the Stara Varoš neighbourhood hides independent galleries and craft shops. Wine lovers should try Podgorica's tasting rooms — Montenegro's Crmnica wine region starts just south of the city.
podgoricaBody.skadarText: The largest lake in the Balkans straddles the Montenegro-Albania border, 30 minutes south of the city. National Park status protects its wetlands, where Dalmatian pelicans, herons, and 270 other bird species breed. Boat tours from Virpazar cost around €15–20 per person. Pack a picnic — lakeside restaurants are limited and overpriced.
podgoricaBody.danilovgradText: A river town 30 minutes west with a mellow vibe. The Zeta River beaches draw locals on hot days. Nearby Zdrebaonik Monastery sits in quiet forest. If you are driving to Nikšić or the mountains, it makes a natural lunch stop.
podgoricaBody.zabljak: Žabljak sits at 1,500 metres in the Durmitor mountain range, about 170 km north of Podgorica. Winter skiing, summer hiking, and the Tara River Canyon — the deepest in Europe — make it a year-round draw. The drive up is spectacular but allow three hours.

podgoricaAirportBody.h1: Pick Up Your Car at Podgorica Airport (TGD)
podgoricaAirportBody.p1: Podgorica Airport handles more international routes than Tivat and stays busier year-round. It sits 65 km from Budva — about an hour's drive, mostly on good dual carriageway. The airport code TGD nods to the city's former name, Titograd.
podgoricaAirportBody.p2: A new terminal opened in 2006, and the original building now serves as an aviation history display. The airport punches above its weight — it once won ACI Europe's award for best airport under 1 million passengers.
podgoricaAirportBody.distIntro: The airport is 12 km south of Podgorica city centre.
podgoricaAirportBody.dist2: Budva: 65 km (approximately 1 hour via Lake Skadar road)
podgoricaAirportBody.dist3: Kotor: 80 km (1 hour 15 minutes via the bay)
podgoricaAirportBody.dist4: Sveti Stefan: 75 km (just over 1 hour heading south-west)
podgoricaAirportBody.flightsText: Daily Belgrade service runs year-round. Seasonal direct flights connect to London, Paris, Rome, Frankfurt, Vienna, Budapest, and more. Budget carriers make TGD the cheaper entry point for many riviera holidays.
podgoricaAirportBody.facilitiesText: Eight departure gates, two arrival gates, a cafe, duty-free, and car rental desks in the arrivals hall. It is small enough that you will not get lost.
podgoricaAirportBody.awardText: Despite its size, Podgorica Airport has taken home the ACI Europe award for the best airport in its category. Efficient processing and friendly staff get consistent praise.
podgoricaAirportBody.rentalText: We meet you at the arrivals hall. No shuttle bus, no queue at a counter. Your car is in the car park right outside. Clear customs, sign the paperwork, and you are on the road. The drive to Budva passes Lake Skadar — keep an eye out for pelicans and pull over at one of the viewpoints.

dubrovnikAirportBody.h1: Fly into Dubrovnik, Drive to the Riviera
dubrovnikAirportBody.p1: Dubrovnik Airport (DBV) often has wider international connections and cheaper flights than Tivat or Podgorica. The trade-off is distance: roughly 2.5 hours of driving to reach Budva. The upside? That drive follows one of Europe's most spectacular coastal roads.
dubrovnikAirportBody.p2: Cross-border rental is straightforward as long as your paperwork is sorted. Tell us at booking that you are starting in Croatia and we make sure the Green Card insurance covers every kilometre.
dubrovnikAirportBody.p3: Plenty of travellers combine a day in Dubrovnik with the drive south. Stop in the old city if you have time — then enjoy the Adriatic views all the way to Budva.
dubrovnikAirportBody.borderText: The border checkpoint at Debeli Brijeg sits between Herceg Novi and Dubrovnik. In summer, queues build fast — peak waits hit 1–2 hours on weekend afternoons. Cross before 8am or after 8pm and you will breeze through. Have your passport, rental contract, and Green Card accessible, not buried in a suitcase.
dubrovnikAirportBody.facilitiesText1: A modern terminal less than 30 minutes from Dubrovnik's old city walls on the E65. Parking fills quickly in peak summer — pre-booking saves stress.
dubrovnikAirportBody.facilitiesText2: Inside: bureau de change, ATMs, post office, restaurants, duty-free, and a newsstand. Everything you need before hitting the road south.
dubrovnikAirportBody.gettingText1: Leave the airport and join the coastal E65 heading south. You will cross the Montenegro border at Debeli Brijeg, then follow the bay road through Herceg Novi and along the Boka Kotorska.
dubrovnikAirportBody.gettingText2: Two and a half hours of Adriatic coastline, one border stop, and you pull into Budva. On a clear day this ranks among the best drives in southern Europe. Keep the camera charged.

montenegroBody.h1: Driving Montenegro from a Budva Base
montenegroBody.p1: Montenegro packs dramatic variety into a tiny footprint. Twenty-one kilometres of riviera beach. A fjord-like bay ringed by medieval towns. A lake with 270 bird species. Mountain canyons deeper than anything in the Alps. And you can reach all of it from Budva in under three hours. A rental car turns a beach week into a proper adventure.
montenegroBody.p2: Fair warning: distances on the map lie. Mountain switchbacks, single-lane coast roads, and the occasional goat crossing mean real driving times run 30–50% longer than Google suggests. Lean into it. The slow bits are usually the most scenic bits.
montenegroBody.startText: Most visitors pick up at Tivat Airport (22 km, 30 min) or Podgorica Airport (65 km, 1 hr). Dubrovnik Airport in Croatia works too — 2.5 hours via the coast road. Once in Budva, you are centre-stage on the riviera with fast access north to the bay and south toward Bar and Albania.
montenegroBody.timesText1: These times assume summer traffic and no major stops. Add 15–20 minutes on weekend afternoons for the Budva–Kotor tunnel queue.
montenegroBody.timesText2: Sveti Stefan: 15 min. Kotor: 30 min. Tivat: 30 min. Petrovac: 20 min. Herceg Novi: 1 hr. Podgorica: 1 hr. Dubrovnik: 2.5 hrs. Žabljak: 3 hrs.
montenegroBody.rulesIntro: Police stops are common but painless if your documents are in order. Keep these in the car:
montenegroBody.rule1: Valid driving licence (international permit accepted alongside your home licence)
montenegroBody.rule2: Rental contract — the original, not a screenshot
montenegroBody.rule4: Green Card if you plan to cross any border (around €15 for 15 days)
montenegroBody.keyRule1: Seatbelts mandatory, front and back, every trip
montenegroBody.keyRule2: Phone use while driving is banned — hands-free only
montenegroBody.keyRule3: Alcohol limit is zero. Not 'one glass'. Zero.
montenegroBody.keyRule4: Speed cameras are real and fines arrive in the post
montenegroBody.conditionsText: Main routes are well-surfaced. The E65 coast road and the Budva–Podgorica dual carriageway are smooth. Mountain roads get narrower, steeper, and occasionally lack barriers. Some local drivers treat centre lines as suggestions. Stay alert on blind corners, especially between Budva and Petrovac where the road hugs the cliffs.
montenegroBody.e65Title: North: The Bay of Kotor Road
montenegroBody.e65Text: Through the tunnel to Kotor, then along the water's edge past Perast, Risan, and on to Herceg Novi. This is the scenic route that ends up on everyone's Instagram. Stop at a waterfront konoba in Perast for lunch. Total loop back to Budva: about 3 hours with stops.
montenegroBody.e762Text: Coast south through Petrovac and Bar to Ulcinj, near the Albanian border. Or peel inland at Podgorica for the mountains — Durmitor National Park, Žabljak, and the Tara Canyon (deepest in Europe at 1,300m). The inland route is longer but completely transforms what you think Montenegro looks like.
montenegroBody.borderText: Montenegro borders five countries, and you can drive to all of them with the right paperwork. The Green Card covers insurance. The rental contract needs to list every country you plan to visit. Summer weekends bring long queues at the Croatian coastal crossing — weekday mornings or late evenings are the way to go.
montenegroBody.nationTitle: A Tiny Country, Full Sized Scenery
montenegroBody.nationText: Independent only since 2006, Montenegro is one of Europe's youngest nations. Tourism has boomed in the years since. Budva leads the way, attracting a third of all visitors, but the rest of the country is catching up fast. There has never been a better time to rent a car and see what the whole place offers.

borderCrossingBody.h1: Taking Your Rental Car Across Borders
borderCrossingBody.p1: Yes, you can drive your rental car out of Montenegro. Cross-border travel is permitted to Croatia, Bosnia & Herzegovina, Albania, Kosovo, and Serbia. The key: tell your rental company which countries you plan to visit when you book. They prepare the paperwork so you are covered at every checkpoint.
borderCrossingBody.greenCardText: A Green Card is an international motor insurance certificate. Border guards check it to confirm your vehicle is insured in the country you are entering. No card, no crossing. Simple as that.
borderCrossingBody.greenCard1: Cost: around €15 for 15 days of cover
borderCrossingBody.greenCard2: Your rental company arranges it — just confirm the countries at booking
borderCrossingBody.greenCard3: Covers: Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Kosovo, and Serbia
borderCrossingBody.greenCardWarn: Check your Green Card covers every country on your route before you leave. A card valid for Croatia will not get you into Albania. Border guards check carefully.
borderCrossingBody.docsTitle: What You Need at the Border
borderCrossingBody.doc2: Driving licence (international licence accepted alongside your national one)
borderCrossingBody.doc3: Original rental contract — not a photocopy, not a phone screenshot
borderCrossingBody.doc5: Vehicle registration document (stays in the car)
borderCrossingBody.croatiaText: The coast road crossing at Debeli Brijeg between Herceg Novi and Dubrovnik is the most popular and the most congested. Summer weekend queues hit 1–2 hours at peak times. This is the one that catches people out.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaTip: From Budva: allow 1 hour to reach the border. Best windows: before 8am or after 8pm. Weekdays are dramatically faster than weekends.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaFerry: Time-saver: the Kamenari–Lepetane car ferry cuts across the Bay of Kotor in 10 minutes, shaving 45 minutes off the drive around the bay. Runs every 15 minutes, costs around €4.50 per car.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaText: Two main crossings: Sukobin/Muriqan on the coast near Ulcinj, or Hani i Hotit inland near Lake Skadar. The coastal route is easier and more scenic. Budget 2–3 hours from Budva to Shkodër.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaNote: Heads up: Albanian roads are rougher. Livestock on the road is common outside cities. Drive cautiously, especially at dusk. Fuel up before crossing — petrol stations are spaced further apart.
borderCrossingBody.bosniaText: The main crossing at Šćepan Polje (near Foča) connects to the Drina valley and Mostar beyond. Alternatively, cross at Vilusi from Nikšić. Both crossings are quiet with minimal waits year-round.
borderCrossingBody.serbiaText: Cross at Dobrakovo on the E65 north of Bijelo Polje. This connects to the Serbian motorway network and Belgrade. Allow 6 hours from Budva with stops.
borderCrossingBody.kosovoText: The Kula crossing near Rožaje connects to Peja/Peć in Kosovo. Double-check that Kosovo is listed on your Green Card — some policies exclude it.
borderCrossingBody.tipsTitle: How to Make Border Crossings Painless
borderCrossingBody.tip1: Stack all documents on the dashboard before you reach the queue. Fumbling at the window slows everyone down
borderCrossingBody.tip2: Kill the engine while waiting — border guards notice and appreciate it
borderCrossingBody.tip3: Avoid summer weekends at the Croatian coast crossing. Weekdays cut your wait by 80%
borderCrossingBody.tip4: Inland crossings to Bosnia and Serbia are almost always faster than the coastal Croatia route
borderCrossingBody.tip5: Carry cash in euros. Useful for tolls, snacks, and the occasional roadside watermelon seller on the other side
borderCrossingBody.tip6: Check your mobile roaming plan covers the next country. Data charges at borders are a common surprise
borderCrossingBody.lawsTitle: Traffic Laws Change at the Border
borderCrossingBody.lawsIntro: Every Balkan country has slightly different road rules. The differences that catch drivers out:
borderCrossingBody.law1: Croatia: headlights on at all times, even in broad daylight. Motorway tolls apply — card or cash at booths
borderCrossingBody.law2: Albania: lower speed limits on rural roads. Road quality drops sharply outside main cities
borderCrossingBody.law3: Bosnia: winter tyres mandatory November through April. First-aid kit and warning triangle must be in the car
borderCrossingBody.law4: Serbia: zero alcohol tolerance for all drivers. Motorway tolls are charged at booth gates — collect a ticket when you join, settle the fare when you leave
borderCrossingBody.law5: Kosovo: drive on the right. International driving permit recommended. Road signage is improving but still patchy in rural areas
borderCrossingBody.route1: Budva → Dubrovnik: 2.5 hrs on the coastal E65. One border crossing, spectacular scenery the entire way
borderCrossingBody.route2: Budva → Mostar: 4 hrs via Trebinje. The Stari Most bridge alone makes this drive worthwhile
borderCrossingBody.route3: Budva → Shkodër: 3 hrs via the Ulcinj coastal crossing. Albania's lakeside city with a hilltop fortress
borderCrossingBody.route4: Budva → Belgrade: 6 hrs via the E65 north through Podgorica. Motorway most of the way once you cross into Serbia
borderCrossingBody.ctaText: Need a car that is ready for borders? Search our fleet, tick the countries you plan to visit, and we handle the Green Card. It will be waiting in the glovebox at pickup.
